data.title: GE Aerospace Q4 earnings preview: LEAP engine demand, CES growth in focus
data.description:
GE Aerospace (GE) is set to announce fourth quarter earnings on Thursday, and investors closely watch for the company’s LEAP engine program, commercial engines and services (CES) growth, as well as its backlog amid surging demand for its LEAP engine platform.

Wall Street expects the aircraft engine maker to post EPS of $1.43, implying an 8.3% year-over-year increase, while revenue is expected to rise 13.5% to $11.21B during the quarter.

The company, in its Q3 earnings call, raised its 2025 EPS guidance to $6-$6.20 on the back of elevated demand and higher services revenue.

At the Baird 55th Annual Global Industrial Conference, Rahul Ghai, Senior VP & CFO, mentioned that on the OE side, the company expects its LEAP engine deliveries to grow next year. It will also increase its volumes on the 9X platform. Additionally, on DPT, it has $19B of backlog that supports good growth.

RBC Capital Markets maintained its Outperform rating, increasing its price target for GE to $355 from $340.

“GE remains one of the highest quality A&D stocks and should benefit from a position of strength with its growing LEAP installed base (long-term services upside) and widebody engine portfolio, and we expect continued near-term upside from the engine AM from the legacy NB AM (CFM56) given the continued undersupply of new aircraft,” RBC analyst Ken Herbert said.

In the past year, GE Aerospace shares gained nearly 69%, outperforming the broader S&P 500 Index, which gained 12.3% during the same period.

Seeking Alpha's Quant rating recommended the stock as a Hold, while Seeking Alpha analysts and Wall Street analysts are bullish, rating the company as a Buy.

Susquehanna also gave a Positive rating to GE, increasing its price target to $380 from $350.

The brokerage firm, in its research report, highlighted that GE continues to see robust defense demand, driven by NATO commitments of higher spending, as well as robust commercial aftermarket due to expectations of ongoing RPK growth. However, the firm lowered its 2026-28 estimates because of 777X-associated headwinds.

Over the last two years, GE has beaten EPS estimates 100% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 63% of the time.

Over the last three months, EPS estimates have seen 11 upward revisions versus one downward move, while revenue estimates have seen 11 upward revisions and no downward revision.

data.title: Goldman Sachs names new chair of investment banking - report
data.description:
Goldman Sachs (GS) promoted Ben Frost, the firm's top consumer retail banker, to chairman of investment banking, after he led the bank on two of the sector's biggest deals in the past year, according to a media report.

Reuters reported the promotion on Wednesday, citing an internal memo. A person familiar with the matter confirmed the report to Seeking Alpha.

Frost, who joined Goldman in 2018, was most recently co-head of the global consumer and retail group, part of the investment banking unit. In 2025, the bank advised Kenvue on its ~$50B deal to be acquired by Kimberly-Clark (KMB) and Sycamore Partners in its ~$24B of Walgreens Boots Alliance.

A separate memo named Cosmo Roe and Milan Hasecic as new co-heads of the global consumer and retail group.

Goldman Sachs (GS) declined to comment to Seeking Alpha.

Goldman Sachs stock rose 2.0% in late morning trading on Wednesday.

data.title: Ally Financial outlines 2026 NIM target of 3.6%-3.7% with capital discipline and share repurchases amid strategic focus
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ally Financial Inc. (ALLY) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Michael Rhodes, CEO, highlighted a strategic refresh undertaken in 2025, stating the company has “delivered solid results” through deliberate choices and disciplined execution. Rhodes emphasized a focused strategy, investing in businesses where Ally has “clear competitive advantages and a reason to win.” He reported adjusted EPS of $3.81, up 62% year-over-year, and core ROTCE of 10.4%, up more than 300 basis points versus the previous year. Rhodes noted, “Retail net charge-offs ended the year below 2%,” and highlighted expense and capital discipline, aiming for net interest margin (NIM) “in the upper 3% range.” He also announced a $2 billion open-ended share repurchase authorization.

  • Rhodes explained that Retail Auto and Corporate Finance loans increased 5% in 2025, with $43.7 billion in consumer loan originations at a 9.7% origination yield, and 43% of the volume in the highest credit quality tier. He pointed to record application volumes and growth in SmartAuction and Passthrough programs as contributors to durable fee growth. Insurance written premiums exceeded $1.5 billion, and the company ended the year with $144 billion in retail deposit balances, serving 3.5 million customers.

  • CFO Russell Hutchinson reported net financing revenue, excluding OID, of $1.6 billion, up 6% year-over-year, and adjusted other revenue of $550 million, down 2% year-over-year, excluding a $27 million loss from moving legacy mortgage assets to held for sale. Hutchinson commented, “Fourth quarter adjusted provision expense of $486 million was down $71 million year-over-year, largely driven by continued improvement in retail auto NCOs as well as the exit from the credit card business.” He also noted GAAP and adjusted EPS for the quarter were $0.95 and $1.09, respectively.

Outlook

  • Hutchinson provided 2026 guidance, stating, “We expect full year NIM between 3.6% and 3.7% -- the range for NIM reflects the evolving path of interest rates as the Fed easing cycle continues with 2 cuts assumed for 2026.” He also guided for retail auto net charge-offs between 1.8% and 2% for the year, and consolidated net charge-offs between 1.2% and 1.4%. Hutchinson added, “On expenses, we expect 2026 to be up approximately 1% with investment focused on our core franchises.” He reaffirmed the company’s goal of achieving its mid-teens return target through “an upper 3s NIM; two, a sub-2% retail auto NCO rate; and three, capital and expense discipline.”

Financial Results

  • NIM, excluding OID, was 3.51% for Q4, down 4 basis points from the prior quarter, resulting in a full year NIM of 3.47%. Adjusted net revenue was $8.5 billion, up 3% year-over-year and up 6% when adjusting for the sale of card. CET1 ended the year at 10.2%, with fully phased-in CET1 at 8.3%. Ending balances across retail auto and Corporate Finance were up $5 billion year-over-year. Retail auto net charge-offs for the quarter were 2.14%, down 20 basis points year-over-year. Adjusted noninterest expense was $1.2 billion, excluding a $31 million restructuring charge.

  • Insurance core pretax income was $89 million for the quarter, with total written premiums of $384 million. Corporate Finance delivered core pretax income of $98 million, with a Q4 ROE of 29% and full year ROE of 28%.

Q&A

  • Robert Wildhack, Autonomous Research: Asked about NIM progression and retail auto coverage ratio. CFO Hutchinson noted, “NIM progression is not a straight line ... we feel confident in terms of the medium trajectory around NIM.” On reserves, he explained, “Reserve releases is not something that we factor into how we think about the business from a return perspective.”

  • Sanjay Sakhrani, KBW: Asked about 2026 risks and opportunities. CEO Rhodes said, “I do feel a lot of optimism for '26. And it's anchored on the fundamentals of the business ... '26 will be about building strong volumes with the right margins.” CFO Hutchinson added, “We've taken deliberate steps to reduce credit risk, to reduce rate risk and to increase capital.”

  • Mark DeVries, Deutsche Bank: Inquired about NIM upper bound and ROE trajectory. Hutchinson stated, “Upper 3s, I think we've talked about it previously as being -- we talked about kind of 4% back when we still had the card business ... one outstanding item is getting NIM to the high 3s.”

  • Jeffrey Adelson, Morgan Stanley: Asked about S-Tier origination mix and capital management. Hutchinson responded, “We're continuously tweaking our approach to underwriting to make it better ... flattish portfolio yield is made it to declining cost of deposits which is obviously an important driver of NIM expansion.” On buybacks, he said, “Low and slow, but doing share repurchases alongside all the other capital priorities ... as we get through that 9% build, our share repurchase level will accelerate.”

  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs: Asked about buyback pacing and core ROTCE methodology. Hutchinson replied, “Having this share repurchase authorization in place is something that gives us flexibility ... this is an updated methodology. It in no way alters our mid-teens return target.”

  • Moshe Orenbuch, TD Cowen: Asked about competitive dynamics. Hutchinson said, “I feel really good about where the franchises are, in particular, our dealer financial services franchise.” Rhodes added, “The competition was more intense this year, but incredibly proud of this team. This whole year has been about strategic pivots backed by disciplined execution.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on NIM progression, reserve releases, buyback pacing, and competitive dynamics, expressing cautious optimism but probing for clarity on macro risks and capital returns. Tone was neutral to slightly positive, with analysts seeking confirmation of management’s confidence in guidance and strategy.

  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, especially during prepared remarks. During Q&A, their responses were measured, occasionally reiterating prudent risk management and discipline, but consistently expressed optimism for 2026 and beyond. Rhodes stated, “I feel very good about how we're positioned,” and Hutchinson emphasized, “We feel confident in terms of the medium trajectory around NIM.”

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained steady, while management’s confidence increased, particularly with the initiation of share repurchases and progress on strategic objectives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 NIM increased to a range of 3.6%-3.7% from the previous quarter’s full-year guide of 3.45%-3.5%.

  • Management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism to pronounced confidence, underpinned by improved credit trends, progress on capital build, and new share repurchase authorization.

  • Analysts continued to focus on NIM, credit trends, and capital returns, but questions reflected heightened interest in capital deployment and competitive positioning due to the evolving macro backdrop.

  • The company reiterated progress on expense control and risk management, building on previous quarter’s strategic pivots and streamlining.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged macroeconomic uncertainty, particularly the labor market and used vehicle values, as key risks influencing credit performance and reserve strategy.

  • Hutchinson indicated, “Unemployment over the course of 2026 is going to be higher than the unemployment that we saw over the full year of 2025,” impacting reserve levels and net charge-off expectations.

  • Analysts raised questions about the pace of reserve releases and buybacks, probing the impact of competitive pressures and execution risk on guidance.

Final Takeaway

Ally Financial’s leadership emphasized that 2025 marked a meaningful step forward, with disciplined execution of a focused strategy, improved credit trends, robust capital levels, and expense control. Management underscored confidence in achieving a 2026 NIM of 3.6%-3.7%, continued risk-adjusted growth in core businesses, and resumed share repurchases as a sign of progress and optimism for long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Lexicon Pharma spikes after FDA feedback on pain drug
data.description:
• Lexicon Pharmaceuticals (LXRX) traded sharply higher on Wednesday in reaction to positive updates from a meeting between the company and the U.S. FDA regarding the late-stage development of pilavapadin, its experimental therapy for diabetic peripheral neuropathic pain.
  • During the End-of-Phase 2 meeting, the regulator didn’t oppose the company’s plans to advance the once-daily, oral therapy into Phase 3 development, the Woodlands, Texas-based biopharma said.
  • Lexicon’s (LXRX) late-stage plans for pilavapadin include two placebo-controlled, 12-week, registrational studies designed to evaluate a 10 mg daily dose of the drug versus placebo. The 12-week change of average daily pain score will serve as the primary endpoint of the trials.
  • Meanwhile, CEO Mike Exton noted that the FDA hasn’t required the company to conduct additional preclinical or clinical studies that could potentially delay or complicate pilavapadin’s late-stage development and its planned regulatory submission.

data.title: TE Connectivity signals double-digit EPS growth for 2026 amid record $5.1B orders and AI momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TE Connectivity (TEL) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Terrence Curtin, CEO, emphasized the company’s continued investment in growth drivers tied to increased customer demand for data and power connectivity. He stated, “we have been investing and have broadened our growth drivers to benefit from secular trends that are driven by the increased needs by our customers around data and power connectivity.”
  • Curtin highlighted record orders of over $5 billion, reflecting growth of more than $1 billion versus the prior year, fueled by new program awards and broad-based momentum across businesses. “Our first quarter results and our expectations going forward to reinforce these key messages that we convey. We delivered over 20% sales growth in the first quarter with growth in both segments by driving content growth above market.”
  • The CEO outlined record adjusted earnings per share of $2.72, which increased over 30% year-over-year, and record adjusted operating margins of 22%.
  • Curtin noted ongoing improvements in supply chain localization and operational resilience, reinforcing the company’s ability to deliver “record adjusted operating margins and earnings per share in the first quarter, along with strong cash generation.”
  • He also signaled an upward revision in AI revenue expectations for fiscal 2026: “We now expect our AI revenues in fiscal 2026 to be a couple of hundred million dollars higher than our view 90 days ago, with growth expected across every hyperscale customer.”
  • Heath Mitts, CFO, reported, “For the quarter, we achieved record adjusted operating income of over $1 billion with an adjusted operating margin of 22% driven by strong operational performance by our teams.”

Outlook

  • Curtin reported, “As we look forward, we are expecting our second quarter sales to be $4.7 billion, reflecting an increase of 13% year-over-year on a reported basis and 6% organically. We expect adjusted earnings per share to be around $2.65, and this is 20% growth year-over-year.”
  • Sequential growth is expected in the Industrial Solutions segment, with transportation seeing typical auto seasonality trends. Management reiterated that growth for fiscal 2026 is expected to be ahead of the long-term target of 6 to 8 points of annual average growth.

Financial Results

  • First quarter sales were reported at $4.7 billion, representing 22% reported and 15% organic year-over-year growth, with both segments exceeding prior guidance.
  • Orders increased to $5.1 billion, with a book-to-bill ratio of 1.1. Adjusted earnings per share were $2.72. Adjusted operating margins reached 22%, an increase of 180 basis points from the previous year.
  • Free cash flow surpassed $600 million, and all free cash flow was returned to shareholders during the quarter through share buybacks and dividends. Cash from operations was $865 million.
  • The Industrial Solutions segment grew sales by 38% (26% organically), with digital data networks growing 70% and AI revenue outperforming prior expectations. The segment’s adjusted operating margins expanded by over 500 basis points to 23%.
  • Transportation segment sales grew 10% (7% organically), with auto sales up 7% organically. Commercial transportation achieved 16% organic growth year-over-year.
  • CapEx for the current year is expected to approach 6% of sales to support the growing AI pipeline.

Q&A

  • Scott Davis, Melius Research: Asked for confirmation on the AI revenue forecast increase and more detail on scaling capacity and margins. Curtin confirmed the $200 million increase and said, “as we continue to build the momentum, the orders that we just talked about were very strong…we have been improving the margins in IS across all the businesses.”
  • Mark Delaney, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about order trends and revenue implications. Curtin explained the record $5.1 billion in orders as “very broad-based,” noting, “in Transportation, our orders are reflecting what we see in production patterns…there will be partially offset by auto production in the world, which will be down about 3 million units.”
  • Amit Daryanani, Evercore: Sought clarity on drivers of the AI revenue uptick and investment needs. Curtin attributed it to new hyperscaler program awards, while Mitts discussed accelerating CapEx tied to specific program wins.
  • Wamsi Mohan, BofA Securities: Queried on the nature of AI programs and content split. Curtin responded, “these are hyperscaler programs…it is across power and data and signal...it is broad across both spectrums as we move to next-generation architectures.”
  • Luke Junk, Baird: Asked about trends in ACL and incremental margins. Curtin called out a multi-year downturn now reversing, with “the CapEx side of it, and we’re seeing it broadly across all regions.” Mitts added confidence in 30%+ flow-through math for both segments.
  • Multiple analysts probed on supply chain, inflation, and capacity. Curtin and Mitts detailed that inflation, especially in metals, is being passed through via pricing, with no current memory-related supply constraints affecting TE or its customers.
  • Additional questions covered order duration, segment margin convergence, and the outlook for commercial transportation, with management reiterating confidence in ongoing growth and operational leverage.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the sustainability of AI-driven growth, order momentum, and incremental margins, generally expressing a positive but probing tone, especially around scaling, capacity, and order visibility.
  • Management’s tone was confident and assertive, particularly regarding AI growth, operational leverage, and the company’s ability to pass on inflationary costs. Curtin stated, “we feel very good about the momentum. The orders reflect it. The DDN orders were up 70% year-over-year in the quarter, which is very strong.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence was elevated, especially around broadening growth drivers and stronger-than-expected AI contributions. Analyst tone was similarly upbeat but continued to test management on sustainability and order conversion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q2 2026 points to $4.7 billion in sales and $2.65 EPS, both representing robust double-digit year-over-year growth, following Q1’s $4.7 billion in sales and $2.72 EPS.
  • Management increased its AI revenue target for 2026 by $200 million compared to the prior quarter’s projection.
  • Both quarters featured strong order growth, but Q1 saw a more substantial record in overall orders and book-to-bill ratio, with continued momentum in digital data networks and energy.
  • The tone shifted from emphasizing resilience and stability in Q4 to a more assertive outlook in Q1, with greater focus on broad-based growth and the acceleration of AI-related investments.
  • Analysts’ questions evolved from clarifying growth sources and margin expansion to examining the durability of AI momentum and the company’s ability to manage inflation and supply chain risks.
  • Management’s confidence in incremental margins and AI program scaling increased versus the previous quarter, and guidance for CapEx was raised to support growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Inflationary pressures, especially in metals, were singled out as a business risk, with Mitts stating, “we are seeing pressure…on the metals specifically. That category is our largest purchase category. So the team is hyper focused.”
  • Potential seasonality in auto production impacting transportation segment sequential performance was discussed.
  • Management reported no current supply chain constraints in memory or other critical components, but is closely monitoring market conditions and passing through inflationary costs as needed.
  • Analysts raised concerns about capacity to meet accelerating AI demand, but management expressed confidence in the ability to ramp production and investments in tooling and facilities.

Final Takeaway

TE Connectivity delivered a record-setting Q1 2026, marked by double-digit sales and EPS growth, record orders, and a significant upward revision in AI revenue expectations for the year. Management expects robust performance to continue, underpinned by broad-based order strength, continued investment in digital data networks and energy, and proactive measures to manage inflation and supply chain risks. Fiscal 2026 is projected to deliver growth ahead of the company’s long-term targets, with both segments contributing to margin expansion and strong cash generation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Simmons First National targets low to mid-single-digit loan growth and stable NIM for 2026 while focusing on core deposits
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Simmons First National Corporation (SFNC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jay Brogdon, President and CEO, highlighted a strong quarter for loan growth, stating "the highest level of production, I think, that we've seen in at least a couple of years," while noting this was achieved despite elevated paydowns and some seasonal softness on the agri and mortgage warehouse side.
  • Brogdon indicated the outlook for 2026 is "low to mid-single-digit growth" for loans, driven by a robust pipeline and ongoing caution regarding credit and underwriting standards. He emphasized a focus on both profitability and risk discipline, saying "we're still going to be very, very cautious around the credit and underwriting environment, and then also just from a pricing and profitability perspective."
  • CFO Charles Hobbs broke down net interest margin (NIM) performance, stating "of that 31 basis points, about 19, call it, 19 to 20 of that is from the partial quarter impact of the balance sheet restructure that we did last quarter. But then the rest of that is really from core NIM expansion."
  • Brogdon described the opportunity to exceed guidance as tied to organic growth in low-cost deposits: "Our biggest opportunity to even further exceed the guide is really built around our ability to organically grow some low-cost deposits, and we're very, very focused on that."

Outlook

  • Management guided for loan growth in 2026 in the "low to mid-single-digit" range, with Brogdon stating "our outlook for something that's probably a little more optimistic in loan growth than what we've had over the last couple of years."
  • The NIM outlook for 2026 is described as "relatively stable," with Hobbs stating, "as you think about the full year, we're probably pretty stable, maybe a couple of basis points to get to the mid-3.80s by fourth quarter."
  • The company expects continued NII growth in the 9% to 11% range, supported by loan repricing tailwinds and deposit beta moderation.

Financial Results

  • The reported net interest margin for Q4 was 3.81%, with linked quarter margin growth of 31 basis points, attributed to both balance sheet restructuring and core business improvements.
  • Management noted that expenses in 2025 were below the run rate for expenses in Q4 2022, despite inflation and merit increases, attributing this to disciplined execution of efficiency initiatives.
  • Brogdon highlighted that revenue for Q4 was up almost 20% compared to the previous year and pre-provision net revenue increased 60% year-over-year.

Q&A

  • Matt Olney, Stephens Inc.: Asked about the drivers of loan growth and pipeline strength. Brogdon responded that the quarter featured the highest production in years, and the approved, ready-to-close pipeline is at a multi-quarter high, though overall pipeline is flat, indicating confidence for early 2026 growth but caution for sustained pace.
  • Olney inquired about margin sustainability. Hobbs explained the 3.81% NIM, with tailwinds from loan repricing and balance sheet restructuring, and forecasted stability in the NIM for 2026, assuming rate cuts in May and August.
  • Olney sought color on market competition. Brogdon noted deposit competition comes mostly from smaller banks, while irrational loan pricing, especially in CRE, is a greater challenge, but not enough to discourage the growth outlook.
  • David Feaster, Raymond James: Asked about asset quality and divestitures. Brogdon emphasized stable credit, successful resolution of problem credits, and cleanup of legacy nonperformers, with no new divestitures planned.
  • Feaster queried on hiring and process improvements. Brogdon described strong talent pipelines and continued progress in efficiency and automation, supported by controlled expense growth.
  • Wood Lay, KBW: Asked if higher loan production reflects increased aggressiveness or customer optimism. Brogdon attributed it primarily to improved opportunities rather than lowered standards.
  • Lay questioned the NIM range, with Hobbs and Brogdon agreeing the upper end may have shifted up due to higher rates.
  • Lay inquired about capital deployment. Brogdon prioritized organic growth and dividends, with potential for share buybacks if conditions warrant.
  • Brian Wilczynski, Morgan Stanley: Asked about ROTCE trajectory. Brogdon and Hobbs pointed to a strong quarter but highlighted seasonality and tax effects, aiming for a sustainable mid-teens ROTCE and ROA of 1.25% and above.
  • Wilczynski asked about deposit growth strategy. Brogdon outlined ongoing consumer banking, private banking, and commercial C&I initiatives, while COO Chris Steenberg added focus on small business deposit growth.
  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson: Queried about the loan-deposit ratio and funding growth. Brogdon stated core deposit growth is the main constraint, with other funding options considered only after maximizing customer deposits.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on growth drivers, margin sustainability, competitive pressures, credit quality, hiring, expense management, and capital allocation, with a generally positive but probing tone, pressing for specifics on sustainability and competitive responses.
  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone throughout, emphasizing stability, strong pipelines, and opportunity, while acknowledging ongoing challenges in competition and funding. Brogdon stated, "we are nowhere near satisfied with where we are right now" and highlighted continued strategic initiatives.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the current tone is more confident, with management referencing significant progress and stronger momentum, while analysts remain focused on sustainability and execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The outlook for loan growth shifted from low single-digit to low to mid-single-digit, reflecting increased optimism due to pipeline strength and higher production.
  • NIM guidance moved from a range of 3.50%–3.75% to a more stable mid-3.80% expectation, attributed to balance sheet repositioning and rate environment.
  • Analysts continued to focus on competitive dynamics, deposit growth, and expense control, but with increased attention to capital deployment and ROTCE.
  • Management’s confidence was notably higher, stressing improved profitability, balance sheet strength, and strategic execution compared to the previous quarter’s more cautious tone following restructuring.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted persistent competitive pressures in loan pricing, particularly in CRE, as the most significant headwind.
  • The constraint on growth remains core deposit generation, with Brogdon stating "our constraining factor in our business from a growth perspective today is certainly not loans. It is on the deposit side."
  • Asset quality was described as stable, with legacy nonperformers resolved or addressed, but management remains vigilant in credit discipline.
  • Expense growth is being tightly managed, with ongoing investments in talent and technology funded by efficiency gains.

Final Takeaway

Simmons First National Corporation closed 2025 with robust loan production, stable asset quality, and disciplined expense management, reporting strong year-over-year improvements in revenue and profitability. Management projects stable NIM in the mid-3.80% range and low to mid-single-digit loan growth for 2026, supported by a solid pipeline and ongoing efficiency initiatives. The company remains focused on growing core low-cost deposits, enhancing talent, and maintaining a competitive edge in risk-adjusted returns, positioning itself for further momentum in the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Intel Q4 earnings on deck: What to expect
data.description:
Intel (INTC) is set to post fourth quarter results on Thursday after markets close.

Wall Street expects the chipmaker to post EPS of $0.08 on revenue of $13.39 billion, implying a fall of 6% during the quarter.

Santa Clara, California-based Intel has been losing PC market share to rival AMD for quite some time. However, under CEO Lip-Bu Tan, the company is undergoing a turnaround strategy, which includes drastic cost-cutting measures and a slew of high-profile investments from Nvidia and Softbank last year.

A roughly 10% ownership from the Trump Administration last year, accompanied by a staggering $11.1B investment by the U.S. government also boosted the company’s balance sheet.

Investors and analysts are now more optimistic that the AI boost along with the turnaround strategy will yield result.

“We had been cautious on Intel mainly given overall uncertainty on customer pipeline and execution headwinds in their foundry business while the core business was also lacking visibility on growth drivers,” HSBC analyst Frank Lee said.

However, the brokerage added that it now expects there is an overwhelmingly increasing demand for server CPUs driven by rising agentic AI and sees FY26e server shipments to grow 15%-20% y-o-y.

“Despite supply that remains tight, we still see an upside bias for FQ4 results on PC [and] server demand strength and see Street numbers (which are about in-line w/normal seasonal) as okay for FQ1 (March) guidance — especially off the stronger Dec Q base,” UBS analyst Timothy Arcuri said.

While tightening global memory supply could also pose as an additional challenge to Intel's turnaround, analysts said that “2026 guidance points to renewed growth, margin expansion, and business stabilization.”

Over the last two years, Intel has beaten EPS estimates 63% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 75% of the time.

Over the last three months, EPS estimates have seen 16 upward revisions, compared to 11 downward revisions, while revenue estimates have seen 15 upward revisions versus 16 downward moves.

Seeking Alpha, Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating and Wall Street analysts are all cautious and rated the stock a Hold.

The company has risen over 80% last year, outperforming the nearly 17% rise in the broader S&P 500 index.

data.title: Gen-AI drove mobile app downloads, revenue growth in 2025: Sensor Tower
data.description:
Generative artificial intelligence apps drove mobile download growth during 2025, and for the first time, non-game apps surpassed game apps in total revenue derived from in-app purchases, according to an in-depth report by Sensor Tower.

Global in-app revenue for non-game apps increased by 21% year over year to $85.6B, while such purchases for mobile games inched up 1% to $81.8B. The report, "State of Mobile 2026," used data from Apple's (AAPL) App Store and Google's (GOOG)(GOOGL) Play Store. It did not include data from third-party purchases or revenue from advertisements.

OpenAI's (OPENAI) ChatGPT made significant gains in 2025. Its downloads increased by 148% year over year. In-app purchases (IAP) for ChatGPT surged to $3.4B in revenue, making it the third highest-grossing app in 2025. It only trailed ByteDance's (BDNCE) TikTok and Google One, a cloud storage service, in IAP revenue.

"This momentum reflects a new phase in the AI race," the report said. "Big tech companies such as Google, Microsoft (MSFT), and X are investing heavily in their AI Assistants, expanding use cases and rolling out new features in an effort to challenge the market leader, ChatGPT."

AI assistants led the way in the Gen-AI genre with the most downloads. This was led by ChatGPT, while Google's Gemini and DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) ranked second and third, respectively. xAI's (X.AI) Grok was sixth, while Meta AI (META) and Microsoft Copilot rounded out the top 10.

"In 2025, the top AI publishers, OpenAI and DeepSeek, accounted for nearly 50% of global downloads, up from just 21% in 2023," the report said. "Big tech publishers have also gained share over this period, rising from 14% in 2023 to nearly 30% in 2025. As a result, many early competitors have been crowded out, leaving roughly one-quarter of downloads for the remaining apps among the top 25 by downloads."

Image and video generation features helped propel the success of the top performers among AI apps, such as OpenAI's GPT-4o and Google's nano-banana model.

Mobile game downloads slide in 2025

Downloads of mobile gaming apps declined for the third straight year, with users downloading 50.4B gaming apps in 2025 compared to 54.3B downloads in 2024 and 57.1B in 2023. Despite the download dip, IAP revenue increased to $81.75B in 2025 from $80.7B in 2024.

The games that generated the most IAP revenue globally included Last War: Survival, Whiteout Survival, Royal Match and MONOPOLY GO!

Meanwhile, Roblox (RBLX) dominated web visits in 2025, capturing 74% of all game publisher web visits.

China's Tencent Holdings (TCEHY) ranked No. 1 among mobile game publishers by IAP revenue in 2025 at $7.5B. Scopely and Century Games ranked No. 2 and No. 3 with just over $3B, while Microsoft ranked No. 4 with $3B.

Finance app usage reflects consumer attitude

Among the finance genre, digital wallet and payment apps, such as PhonePe and PayPal (PYPL), along with cryptocurrency apps, such as Binance (BNB-USD), were among the top downloads in 2025.

Downloads for lending and personal finance apps also increased in the U.S. market.

"A modest dip in consumer sentiment in H1 2025 coincided with increased demand for Buy Now, Pay Later; Personal Finance Budgeting & Planning; and Payday Loan apps in the US," the report said.

The top five finance apps downloaded worldwide in 2025 included, in order, PhonePe, PayPal, Navi, Paytm and Google Pay.

Chinese companies dominate retail app downloads

China-based shopping apps dominated the most downloaded retail app category in 2025. Temu (PDD) ranked No. 1, followed by Shein, Amazon Shopping (AMZN), Meesho, Shopee (SE) and Alibaba (BABA). However, Amazon was still used the most among these apps.

"While Temu dominated downloads in 2025, Amazon remained the global leader in usage, averaging 685 million MAUs in Q4 2025," the report said. "Despite this lead, Temu is rapidly narrowing the gap; it surpassed 530 million MAUs following a massive 48% YoY surge in active users."

Among the food and drink category, McDonald's (MCD) was the most downloaded, followed by the food delivery service Blinkit and KFC (YUM). Meanwhile, Uber Eats' (UBER) diversified ad campaign allowed it to take market share from DoorDash (DASH).

Uber was also the most downloaded app among the travel app segment, followed by Google Maps and Waze.

Short dramas captivate video streaming

The number of video streaming app downloads increased by 39% in 2025, along with an 18% gain in total revenue.

"This resurgence is primarily fueled by the 'short drama' phenomenon," the report said. "These snackable-content platforms have dominated the rankings of the fastest-growing apps, led by Kuku TV, ReelShort, and DramaBox."

"While Asia remains the largest market by volume, contributing approximately 54% of global short drama time spent in 2025, Latin America is emerging as the fastest-growing region for engagement," the report added. "In Latin America, short dramas accounted for nearly 16% of total video entertainment time in Q4 2025."

However, in terms of revenue produced, the more traditional streaming services won. Disney+ (DIS) ranked No. 1, followed by HBO Max (WBD), WeTV, Peacock TV (CMCSA), Paramount (PSKY) and Amazon Prime Video (AMZN).

Social media app leaders  

In terms of global revenue, TikTok, YouTube and Snapchat (SNAP) topped the leaderboard. These were followed by Telegram, Kwai, X, Discord, LINE, BIGO LIVE and Meta's Instagram.

"Short-form video usage increased again across leading social apps in 2025," the report said. "While some major platforms, including TikTok, ended the year with a decline in short video's share of time spent, Meta's Instagram and Facebook more than offset this trend. Reels now accounts for a substantial share of engagement—46% of time spent on Instagram and 29% on Facebook."

Mobile app usage continues to tick up

The amount of time people peered at their smartphones and the amount of dollars spent on IAP increased in 2025.

Total IAP across iOS and Google Play climbed 10.6% to $167B. The average user spent 3.6 hours per day on mobile apps, which was a year-over-year increase of 1.1%. Consumers are also using more apps, with the average user utilizing 34 per month, or about two more apps than they did in 2024.

data.title: Lionsgate to create TV series based on English club Manchester United's history
data.description:
English football club Manchester United (MANU) has struck a deal with U.S. media production company Lionsgate (LION) to create a TV show based on the club’s history, according to a report by The Athletic on Wednesday.

The production remains in a developmental phase and has not yet been written or sold to a streaming platform, the report said, but the concept is similar in style to The Crown, the popular historical drama series on Netflix (NFLX) that chronicled the life of the British monarch Queen Elizabeth II.

Sources told the sports news outlet that an agreement has been reached, which will see Manchester United receive a guaranteed sum in the low multi-million pounds in the event the show is produced and sold.

Future royalties will be shared between United and Lionsgate, with the value growing based on the number of seasons, episodes, and the size of any deal that is agreed, the report said.

British television writer and director Jed Mercurio, known for hit UK TV series Bodyguard and Line of Duty and also a United fan since childhood, has been involved in conversations about the project.

data.title: Ormat to invest in startup to advance next-generation geothermal technology
data.description:
Ormat Technologies (ORA) +1.6% in Wednesday's trading after announcing a $25M equity investment in co-leading a $97M Series B funding round for Sage Geosystems, a startup that harnesses energy from hot rocks to generate electricity in meeting surging power demand from artificial intelligence.

Sage plans to pilot its Pressure Geothermal technology at an existing Ormat (ORA) power plant, with the goal of extracting geothermal heat from hot dry rock and accelerating the time to market for next-generation geothermal solutions.

Upon successful completion of the pilot, Ormat (ORA) said it will have the right to develop, build, own and operate geothermal power plants and energy storage projects utilizing Sage's technology.

While geothermal energy meets a small slice of U.S. power demand, the Department of Energy projects that next-generation systems have the potential to provide up to 120 GW of capacity by 2050, enough to power 90M homes.
